sentences.text: Were they what?

No, they were not abusive to me, actually, but they were abusive to these little animals, because he said that in school they were taught that when they rip out the tail, the tail will regrow, you see.
And so he ripped out-- he caught some of these little animals and ripped out the tail.
And I said to him, oh, boy.
What if that hurts for that little-- he said, who are you?
You were the, you know, you have to learn a little bit better.
We have to be prepared for a war.
That was in 1936.
Well, so--
What happens next.
Now, what next happened was the time when the Germans made the first pogrom.
That was in 1938.
1938, they made a Kristallnacht, because they said that a Jew in Paris-- his name was Grynszpan-- was killing one of the German ambassadors.
How-- it was right in the paper.
I read it in the paper, and the next day-- and the next day there was a pogrom.
They came and they broke in the houses, and they broke everything possible and they took--
--father away.

And they wanted to take my me away, but the police what was there said to the people from the party, the SA, not the SS, but that was the SA with the brown shirts, and day said to him, they said, no, no, no, no, no, no.
We cannot take him.
He is not 15 years old.
He is only 13 years old.
You cannot take him, you know?
So they left me, but they took my father.
My father went to Dachau.
And so the police came the next day, and they told my mother, they said, if you have a family living in outside of Germany, you write to these people to make us paper to move to them.
Then they will release my father.
And so I had an uncle in New York.
And we right away wrote my uncle in New York.
And my uncle sent papers that he would take my father and us-- the whole family.
So we showed it-- we brought this paper to the police department, and the police department somehow made the jobs, and my father was released within one week.
And when my father came back, he told us about the camps.
Well, he told me that it was very bad, that they had to stay and-- in nude in the parade and to go to the showers and, you know, and it was very bad.
And they got beaten and make it faster and make it quicker and so on.
Well, I was quite scared.
And I figured, boy, I almost went there too, because the SA man, he grabbed me to go.
And the police didn't let me.
So that's that.
So we tried to make the papers to go to the United States.
But they said there is a quota, and we have to wait the time.
And by the time we waited to go until 1939, the war started.
And so then we couldn't go anymore.
And in 1940--
In Germany, in Mannheim, in our apartment.
No.
No.
My father-- my father couldn't work since 1936, since the-- since the--
--Nuremberg loss.
Well, on assistance from the Jewish community.
They had somehow a fund for people who didn't work, who had no-- no resources, and so we lived on that.
And 1936, we went also to a separate school.
And so we had the Jewish school, and we had all the professors from the university and the doctors, and they all were teaching us.
And so actually we had a very good education.
But for me, it didn't help me much, because you were living under so much fear.
Because 1938 there was the Kristallnacht and-- and then I didn't know if I would go to the camp or we couldn't leave Germany.

And so in 1940, it probably was about August or September 1940, they came to our house early in the morning, and they said you have 15 minutes to take baggage of approximately 50-- 50 pound per person, and you have to go to the K school for assembly.
The arm-- the SR.
The German party from the Nazi party.
And so I packed up 50 pounds--
Yes.
Well, I took some books.
I took the Bible with me.
And I took books mostly.

I had, oh, history books from-- and then I had some-- I had a little-- made a little radio what I made myself, but it never worked after that, because you got to-- you had to be in the big city so that the little crystal radio would work.
But I took that anyhow with me.
And we took some clothes.
And, well, that was it.
And we went down there, and they came with buses.
They loaded us up in buses and, well, but the funny part was that these boys what lived around me, they came and helped us to carry the baggage.
Yes, the Christian boys, they came to help to carry us the baggage and especially there were three boys.
There were three brothers.
We were very close, and they actually cried, and-- especially because I left them my bike.
I-- we didn't know.
I thought that we-- we going to go in the camp, but--
--I didn't.
I heard the stories from my father.
So I was kind of scared, you know.
So I said goodbye to the boys.

Well, you know, the boys actually were very good because I the whole neighborhood knew that I was fixing bikes and fixing things for them, mechanical, and so-- so I did actually very nice for them and they were very nice to me, except, like I said, there were three or four--
Now they loaded up into the buses, and they brought us to the station.
And in the station, they had [GERMAN] person [GERMAN] and we went in there.
And there came one conductor, and he said psht, be quiet.
Don't tell anybody I told you, but it's not going to be bad.
You going to go to South of France.
And you going to go into a family camp in Southern France.
Oh, that-- OK.
So I started to be a little bit more--
Yeah, relaxed.
OK.
The third-- the journey was approximately-- I don't know but maybe three, four days or so until we came to Per-- Perpignan.
And in Perpignan they told us to get out, and we went into Perpignan.
That is in Southern France by the Pyrenees.
And we went out from the station, and they had trucks, not buses.
So we had to go into the trucks, and the trucks, they brought us to the camp.
And that camp was not very good.
Somehow, this is a district where it rains a lot, and there was a lot of mud.

And to go to this barracks where they divided the women from the men and, well, when we went into these barracks, it was kind of awful, because we had to sleep-- we had to sleep on the-- on the floor with some mattresses, but the straw mattresses.
And the mattresses itself, they looked like potato sacks, you know, filled with straw.
The name of the camp was Gurs.
And-- and that camp, we didn't do nothing.
And finally when I one time I visited my mother where she was in her camp, I passed by the hospital.
And it was not actually a real hospital.
It was just a couple of barracks with a Red Cross on it, and inside beds.
At least they had beds-- steel-- steel beds, you know.
Anyhow, and I met one of my professors from the school from the-- from-- from the university, from Heidelberg.
I met him, and I talked to him.
And he asked me, why don't you come and help me?
And I said, could I?
He said yes, sure you can.
And we need a lot of help in the hospital.
And so I said sure.
When do you want me to come?
He said, well, whenever you want.
I said I can come right now.
I just go and say hello to mom, and I come back here.
And he liked it very much, and I liked it very much.
And I learned a lot about medicine and health.
Was he Jewish?
Yes.
He was--
He was Jewish doctor.
Sure, he was one of the--
The guards were French militia from [FRENCH] group.
Well, actually French fascists.
OK.
Oh, they didn't care about us if we lived or died or anything.
They just didn't care.
The poor living condition and--
--poor food and-- but nobody bothered.
And nobody worked.
And the guards, they were there, but they didn't care.
You could talk to them.
They just turned around, looked the other way.
They didn't care one way or another.
But we cared, because when I started to work in that hospital, I have seen so much misery.
That was just awful.
That was terrible.
We had no medicine.
We didn't know what to do.
We had-- people were 'rottening' away, and I had to operate and cut out the rotten meat and put antibiotics on it, what we just had and give them aspirin and everything.
It was awful.
And the smell was terrible.
All Jewish, yeah.
They were all Jewish, and they were all transported in our 'deportated'-- deported from our part of the country, like from [GERMAN], Mannheim, Heidelberg, and all the districts around, all the Jews.
And that come also the professors and doctors and teachers and everybody, you know, we were there.
Well, we stayed there, oh, I would say about-- that was till about [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] summer-- late summer-- in '41.
Late summer in 1941.
And in late summer in 1941, we went to a family camp, they called it.
You have no fences, and it's going to be a lot nicer, and it's not going to be that much rain.
And it is what's called Rivesaltes.
And--
That was-- to Rivesaltes --with the train.
They put us in passenger walk-ons, and we went to Rivesaltes.
Rivesaltes was a camp which had nothing but sand.
That is very close to Marseille.
That's by the Mediterranean.
And actually I made a little mistake before.
Gurs is by [FRENCH] and the Rivesaltes is by Perpignan.
So we could go to Perpignan.
They gave us a pass, and we could go to Perpignan.
The same people.
The same people, the French militia, but they were just the same.
They didn't care one way or another.
Even they were celebrating in their little house.
Almost every night they drank and they laughed and everything else, and they didn't care what the people were doing.
The reason nobody bothers us, there was no reason to run away.
No.
I don't even think that anybody tried to run away at that time, because we had more food and somehow it was very close to Switzerland, and that was in that district there by the Mediterranean.
And somehow they sent in more stuff with the Red Cross.
And so we had more-- we had more medicine and so on.
But I didn't wanted to work anymore in the hospital, because I couldn't-- I couldn't live with that.
And so I became a [GERMAN].
[LAUGHS] I-- we had-- we had a steam machine, and we steamed the clothing of the people periodically.
And I had one guy, he was my boss, and he was about 10, 15 years older than I.
And so we worked pretty good together and, at the meantime, my father got sick with his eyes , and he got blind.
He had the cataract.
And so they took him to the hospital.
And my mother, she-- they took her away also to a Swiss hospital.
So I was alone, but that's fine, and I worked and so on and was good for about I would say maybe-- so that was-- that was about late summer, about two months-- August, September, October.
That was about October.
And in '41 somebody came from a delegation from the occupied part of France to recruit people to work.
So they came down one morning, and we had to get out from the-- from the barracks.
And they looked us over, and they said you, you, you, you, you, you, come this way and fine.
And then they marched us to a different barrack.
Inside that barrack, they somehow had organized different parts.
So we had to undress, and there were two-- I think they were doctors-- they were sitting there on a desk.
And they were checking us over, and so I heard one doctor say to the other, and he said you know-- pointing at me-- he said look at how he looks.
He is quite skinny.
Oh, the other doctor said, oh, don't worry about it.
We give him something good to eat, and he will be able to work.
So I figured, well, that sounds better.
That was in Southern France, still in--
In German, ya.
Well, they were SS doctors, you see.
So I said, oh, that-- to myself, I thought, well, that sounds not too bad, you know.
And so while I was alone, I couldn't say goodbye to my mother.
I couldn't say goodbye to my father, because they didn't let us go anymore.
They gave us different clothes.
They-- we couldn't get that clothes.
They-- we undressed in this room, and we went to the other room.
They checked us over, and then we went this way.
And there were secretaries sitting there, and they were marking down my name and how tall and this and that.
And then I went around this way into another room, and I had different clothes.
And they looked my size, and they gave me good clothes, and gave me nice shoes.
And right from there, we went to another barrack, and they said in about two hours, we give-- we give you to eat.
And the buses will be here, and you will go to the station.
Oh, fine.
So they brought us food-- good food-- and cake and chocolate and fruits and everything and so-- and then we went into the buses, and then they took us out from the station.
And we drove to Northern France, to Brest.
On the way, they were so good to us.
Every time we came to a station, they came to the windows, and they asked if we wanted something to drink, if we wanted something to eat.
And every station, and finally we came down to Brest.
And we came to Brest, and we looked around.
And all we saw was bunkers.
Bunkers and bunkers and bunkers and bunkers.
And we figured, what are we going to do here, you know?
Anyhow, so they brought us to the camp, and they gave us to eat again, and everybody had a bed, and it was very nice.
We only were about four people in a room.
And the next day, we went to work.
And what it was is they were building bunkers for U-boats-- underwater ships, the U-boats.
And all they were doing is pouring cement, lots of cement-- cement, cement.
Every day the trains came in with lots of cement.
My job was to work with about 30 Hollandase. from Holland.
They were free workers.
There were no-- not in a camp, nothing.
They were free workers, and--
No, no.
Christian.
And the-- the smallest one of these Hollanders must have been about six feet, the smallest one, so-- and the tallest one, must be over seven.
And so we came to the-- they called it the [GERMAN], the-- the workplace.
And they greeted us.
Oh, yes, we need people and that, and we have so much work.
But then they looked at us and they said, ah, I'll tell you what.
We were about eight for this-- for this place.
You know, they divided us, you know.
So We were eight for this.
So they said to us, you know, we carry.
You just load.
Load, you only have to take one sack, and we carry as much as we can.
Oh, fine.
It was very nice.
So we loaded them up, and most of them carried four sacks of cement.
I said, how can they do that?
But the food was so good, the only thing we could not get, what they could get, is luxury stuff like ice cream and cakes and puddings and stuff like that.
But they were so nice, they brought us their stuff.
But the food itself was very good.
The vegetables, the soups, and everything was fine.
Who guarded you?
Over there?
SS.
That's the story.
One day on the way home, I heard the SS yelling at one guy.
Now, I don't remember if that was one of our guys or if that was a Russian prisoner or if that was a French prisoner, because over there we all had the same clothes.
And they were yelling at that guy, and they were yelling in German, verrater-- traitor.
You signaled-- you signaled that-- to that English ship or plane.
You signaled, and they were yelling.
And guess what they were doing?
They ripped his clothes off.
There were about four of the SS.
They ripped his clothes off, and they threw him into the cement mixer.
Life.
I saw it with my own eyes.
We just-- we just passed by.
Well, [SIGHS] I don't know.
I trembled.
That's all.
I-- I was scared to look, because they would come after me and say, you know, they're going to throw me in there too, because I saw what they were doing.
I don't know.
And it looks like to me they didn't care what people say, what people see, you see.
But the only thing is, that my workers, the free work, the Hollanders and the French and the ones what were dressed differently there-- they weren't there.

They were about one mile away-- one kilometer away, I would say, because the cement mixers, they were-- the prisoners, they were throwing the cement, what came with the band-- I don't know how you call that-- the transfer, transfer band, you know.
They loaded it up over there and that traveled a long ways.
And they took it off here, and they took it off there, you know.
So--
Like a.
